name: Nike
funFact: The Nike swoosh was designed in 1971 by a design student for a reported flat fee of $35.

name: McDonald's
funFact: The Golden Arches started as real architecture — the actual arches built into 1950s McDonald's restaurants.

name: Pepsi
funFact: Pepsi's 2008 globe redesign reportedly cost over a million dollars, backed by a design document invoking the Golden Ratio.

name: Spotify
funFact: Spotify swapped its green for a much brighter shade in a 2015 rebrand — and designers argued about it for weeks.

name: Cadbury
funFact: Cadbury has fought multi-year legal battles to trademark its exact shade of purple.

name: Google
funFact: The green 'l' in Google's logo deliberately breaks the primary-color pattern — a reminder that Google doesn't follow the rules.

name: BMW
funFact: The BMW roundel's blue and white come from the Bavarian flag — not a spinning propeller, despite the popular legend.

name: Amul
funFact: The polka-dotted Amul girl has fronted one of the world's longest-running ad campaigns — going since 1966.

name: Swiggy
funFact: Swiggy launched in 2014 with a fleet of exactly six delivery partners in one Bengaluru neighbourhood.

name: Zomato
funFact: Zomato began life in 2008 as 'Foodiebay' — a site for scanned office-canteen and restaurant menus in Delhi.

name: Zepto
funFact: Zepto was founded in 2021 by two 19-year-old Stanford dropouts — a 'zepto' is 10⁻²¹, a very small unit of time.

name: Paytm
funFact: Paytm started in 2010 as a website for prepaid mobile recharges — 'Paytm' is short for 'Pay through mobile'.

name: Tata
funFact: The Tata mark's looping shape is meant to suggest a fountain of knowledge — and a tree you can trust to shelter under.

name: MRF
funFact: MRF — Madras Rubber Factory — began in 1946 as a toy-balloon workshop, decades before it ruled Indian tyres and cricket bats.

name: Flipkart
funFact: Flipkart started in 2007 selling books out of a two-bedroom Bengaluru apartment, with a delivery fleet of two founders.

name: Zerodha
funFact: Zerodha mixes 'zero' with 'rodha' — Sanskrit for barrier — and became India's biggest broker without ever buying an ad.

name: CRED
funFact: CRED gates itself behind a 750+ credit score — a bill-payment app styled like a members-only club.

name: PhonePe
funFact: 'Pe' is Hindi for 'on' — PhonePe literally reads 'on the phone', a pun that stuck across India's payment apps.

name: Razorpay
funFact: Razorpay went through Y Combinator in 2015, back when Indian fintech was a hard sell in Silicon Valley.

name: Ola
funFact: Ola began after its founder was stranded mid-trip by a rented car and driver — he rebuilt the idea as cabs you could trust.

name: Jio
funFact: Jio's 2016 launch made mobile data so cheap that India's average price per GB fell to among the lowest on Earth.

name: Airtel
funFact: Airtel's signature tune was composed by A.R. Rahman — for years one of the most-downloaded ringtones in India.

name: Mahindra
funFact: Mahindra began in 1945 assembling Willys Jeeps for Indian roads — the SUV obsession never left.

name: Bajaj
funFact: The Bajaj Chetak scooter once had a waiting list so long that families reportedly booked one at a child's birth.

name: Hero
funFact: Hero became the world's largest two-wheeler maker by volume — having started as a bicycle-parts shop in Ludhiana.

name: Royal Enfield
funFact: Royal Enfield is the oldest motorcycle brand in continuous production anywhere — born British in 1901, made in Chennai today.

name: Maruti Suzuki
funFact: The first Maruti 800 key was handed over by Indira Gandhi in 1983 — the little car put middle-class India on wheels.

name: CEAT
funFact: CEAT stands for Cavi Elettrici e Affini Torino — the tyre giant began life as an Italian electrical-cable company.

name: Parle
funFact: Parle-G is reportedly the world's best-selling biscuit brand — the 'G' meant glucose long before the 'genius' ads.

name: Britannia
funFact: Britannia has been baking since 1892 — it began in a small Calcutta house with a reported investment of ₹295.

name: Haldiram's
funFact: Haldiram's began as a tiny bhujia shop in 1937 Bikaner — a thinner family-recipe sev that became its own category.

name: Dabur
funFact: Dabur compresses its founder's name — 'Da' from Daktar Burman, who delivered Ayurvedic medicines by bicycle in 1884 Calcutta.

name: Godrej
funFact: Godrej made India's first springless lock in 1897 — and later the ballot boxes for independent India's first election.

name: Bata
funFact: India's 'most Indian' shoe brand is actually Czech — Bata arrived from the town of Zlín in 1931 and never left.

name: Asian Paints
funFact: Asian Paints' impish mascot Gattu, the boy with the paint tin, was sketched by cartoonist R.K. Laxman.

name: Nirma
funFact: The twirling girl on every Nirma pack is Nirupama, the founder's daughter — the brand carries her name.

name: IndiGo
funFact: IndiGo's name folds 'India' into 'go' — an on-time obsession made the low-cost airline the country's biggest.

name: Air India
funFact: Air India's first flight in 1932 was flown by JRD Tata himself, carrying airmail from Karachi to Bombay.

name: Myntra
funFact: Myntra began by selling personalized mugs and T-shirts before pivoting into India's fashion giant.

name: Nykaa
funFact: Nykaa — from 'nayika', the heroine — was founded by a 50-year-old investment banker who left banking to sell lipstick.

name: Meesho
funFact: Meesho is short for 'meri shop' — my shop — built for WhatsApp resellers before it ever sold anything itself.

name: Lenskart
funFact: Lenskart's founder delivered early spectacle orders himself around Delhi — the company now makes millions of frames a year.

name: BigBasket
funFact: BigBasket's founders ran an online grocery back in 1999 — a decade too early — then returned and won the second time.

name: Blinkit
funFact: Blinkit spent eight years as Grofers before rebranding around a single promise: groceries in the blink of an eye.

name: MakeMyTrip
funFact: MakeMyTrip survived the dot-com crash by selling US–India tickets to NRIs, years before India booked travel online.

name: OYO
funFact: OYO stands for 'On Your Own' — its founder was a teenager cataloguing budget rooms on backpacking trips.

name: Dream11
funFact: Dream11 became India's first gaming unicorn — and put its logo on the front of the Indian cricket team's jersey.

name: HDFC Bank
funFact: HDFC Bank's red-and-blue box has gone essentially untouched since 1994 — one of India's most valuable brands never rebranded.

name: SBI
funFact: SBI's keyhole mark, introduced in 1971, was designed at NID Ahmedabad — a small circle standing for safe keeping.

name: ICICI Bank
funFact: ICICI began in 1955 as a World Bank-backed lender to Indian industry — retail banking came four decades later.

name: LIC
funFact: LIC's hands-guarding-a-lamp logo pairs with a motto straight from the Bhagavad Gita: 'Yogakshemam Vahamyaham'.

name: boAt
funFact: boAt started in 2016 selling tough charging cables from an apartment — and became one of the world's largest wearable brands.

name: Titan
funFact: Titan's jingle is Mozart's 25th Symphony — and the Tata joint venture broke a government monopoly on watches.

name: Wipro
funFact: Wipro's rainbow flower crowns a company that began in 1945 selling vegetable oil — Western India Products.

name: Infosys
funFact: Infosys was founded by seven engineers on ₹10,000 — famously borrowed from co-founder Narayana Murthy's wife, Sudha.

name: TCS
funFact: TCS predates the industry it now leads — it began in 1968 punching cards for Indian banks and utilities.

name: Zoho
funFact: Zoho famously hires school-leavers through its own in-house university — degrees optional, villages welcome.

name: Hotstar
funFact: Hotstar has held world records for live-stream audiences — cricket finals have crossed 50 million concurrent viewers.

name: Bisleri
funFact: Bisleri began as an Italian brand selling mineral water to 1960s Bombay — in India, its name became the word for bottled water.

name: Maggi
funFact: Maggi is Swiss, born in 1886 — but no country eats more of its two-minute noodles than India.

name: Apple
funFact: The bite in the Apple logo is there for scale — so the fruit is never mistaken for a cherry, says its designer.

name: Microsoft
funFact: Microsoft's four-pane flag arrived only in 2012 — the company spent 25 years before that with just a wordmark.

name: Amazon
funFact: Amazon's arrow runs from A to Z — and doubles as a smile pointed at the customer.

name: Instagram
funFact: Instagram's icon was a detailed Polaroid camera until 2016 — the gradient is a nod to the old rainbow lens stripe.

name: WhatsApp
funFact: WhatsApp had about 55 employees when it sold for $19 billion — roughly $345 million per person.

name: YouTube
funFact: YouTube's first video, 'Me at the zoo', is 19 seconds of elephants — uploaded by a co-founder in April 2005.

name: Netflix
funFact: Netflix once offered to sell itself to Blockbuster for $50 million. Blockbuster passed.

name: LinkedIn
funFact: LinkedIn launched from Reid Hoffman's living room in 2003 and pulled in just 4,500 members its first month.

name: Snapchat
funFact: Snapchat's ghost has a name — Ghostface Chillah — from when the app was still called Picaboo.

name: Uber
funFact: Uber was sketched out after its founders failed to hail a cab in a Paris snowstorm.

name: Airbnb
funFact: Airbnb is short for 'air bed and breakfast' — the founders literally rented air mattresses on their floor during a sold-out conference.

name: PayPal
funFact: PayPal's early employees went on to found or fund Tesla, LinkedIn, YouTube and SpaceX — the 'PayPal Mafia'.

name: Mastercard
funFact: Mastercard dropped its own name from the logo in 2019 — betting the overlapping circles alone were recognizable enough.

name: Visa
funFact: 'Visa' was picked because it sounds the same in nearly every language on Earth.

name: Samsung
funFact: Samsung means 'three stars' in Korean — the company started in 1938 trading dried fish and noodles.

name: Coca-Cola
funFact: The Coca-Cola script was written in 1887 by the company bookkeeper — chosen simply for his fine penmanship.

name: KFC
funFact: Colonel Sanders was a real Kentucky Colonel whose recipe was reportedly rejected over 1,000 times before a restaurant said yes.

name: Burger King
funFact: Burger King's 2021 rebrand deliberately went backwards — reviving the flat, bun-shaped lettering of its 1970s logo.

name: Domino's
funFact: The three dots on the Domino's tile are its first three stores — the plan to add one per new store died very fast.

name: Pizza Hut
funFact: The red roof in Pizza Hut's logo is the actual hut-shaped roof of the first 1958 restaurant in Wichita.

name: Starbucks
funFact: The Starbucks siren is a twin-tailed mermaid from a 16th-century woodcut — her face is kept slightly asymmetric on purpose.

name: Oreo
funFact: Nobody knows what 'Oreo' means — the company has never confirmed any theory about its own name.

name: Red Bull
funFact: Red Bull is a Thai drink first — Krating Daeng, 'red gaur' — licensed by an Austrian who swore by it for jet lag.

name: Adidas
funFact: Adidas is Adi Dassler's name folded together — his feuding brother founded Puma across the river.

name: Puma
funFact: Puma and Adidas split one German family — the brothers' rival factories faced each other across the river Aurach.

name: Reebok
funFact: Reebok is named after the rhebok, a South African antelope — spelled the way the founders found it in a dictionary.

name: Zara
funFact: Zara was almost called 'Zorba' — a nearby bar had the name first, so the founder rearranged the letter molds he'd already bought.

name: H&M
funFact: H&M began in 1947 as 'Hennes' — Swedish for 'hers' — selling only womenswear; the M came with a hunting store it bought.

name: Levi's
funFact: Levi's 1886 patch shows two horses failing to tear the jeans apart — a trademark designed for customers who couldn't read.

name: Lacoste
funFact: The crocodile honors tennis champion René Lacoste's nickname — often called the first logo ever stitched on the outside of clothing.

name: Rolex
funFact: Rolex is owned by a charitable foundation, not shareholders — set up by its founder Hans Wilsdorf in 1945.

name: Toyota
funFact: Toyota's three ellipses contain every letter of the name — and 'Toyoda' became 'Toyota' partly for a luckier stroke count.

name: Honda
funFact: Honda began by bolting surplus wartime radio generators onto bicycles — Soichiro Honda's first 'motorcycle'.

name: Mercedes-Benz
funFact: Mercedes was a customer's daughter's name; the three-pointed star claims engines for land, sea and air.

name: Audi
funFact: Audi is Latin for 'hear!' — founder August Horch's surname means the same in German, but he'd lost the rights to use it.

name: Volkswagen
funFact: Volkswagen means 'the people's car' — and the Beetle it was created to build eventually outsold the Model T.

name: Ferrari
funFact: Ferrari's prancing horse came from a WWI ace's fighter plane — his mother told Enzo it would bring him luck.

name: Porsche
funFact: The Porsche crest carries Stuttgart's rearing horse — the city began as a stud farm, 'Stutengarten'.

name: Tesla
funFact: Tesla's 'T' isn't just a letter — it's a cross-section of an electric motor's rotor, as Elon Musk confirmed.

name: Hyundai
funFact: Hyundai's slanted 'H' is two figures shaking hands — company and customer, mid-deal.

name: IKEA
funFact: IKEA is an acronym of its founder and his childhood home: Ingvar Kamprad, Elmtaryd farm, Agunnaryd village.

name: LEGO
funFact: LEGO compresses 'leg godt' — Danish for 'play well' — and bricks moulded in 1958 still click with today's.

name: Shell
funFact: Shell really did start with seashells — the founder's father imported decorative shells from the East to Victorian London.

name: FedEx
funFact: There's an arrow hidden between the E and the x of FedEx — design's most famous negative-space trick.

name: DHL
funFact: DHL is Dalsey, Hillblom and Lynn — three founders who began by flying shipping papers from San Francisco to Honolulu.
